turns.text: Up to now the Prosecution has not interrogated me.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: When did you join the Hitler Youth?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I joined the Hitler Youth in 1933.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You joined the Hitler Youth in 1933? How old were you at that time?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I was 23.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And in what capacity did you join?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: First as an ordinary member. In September 1933 I became an Unterbannfuehrer in the Hitler Youth.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Unterbannfuehrer in 1933?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes, in September 1933.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Was that a salaried position or an honorary appointment?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: From 1933 to 1935 I worked as a sports instructor in the Hitler Youth.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And in 1935?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: In 1935 I joined the government offices at Potsdam as an expert on PT in schools.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: But that had nothing to do with the Hitler Youth, had it?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: At Potsdam I also commanded the Potsdam unit and local headquarters of the Hitler Youth.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: So you were a civil servant-or rather, an employee of the State and apart from that an honorary leader of the Hitler Youth?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: From 1935 until 1939 I was a civil servant in the government offices at Potsdam and I also commanded the Hitler Youth unit and local headquarters at Potsdam in an honorary capacity.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Therefore in the summer of 1939 you joined the Reich Youth Leadership, did you?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: In June 1939 I joined the Reich Youth Leadership and became adjutant to Baldur van Schirach who was Reich Youth Leader at the time.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And how long did you hold that of lice?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Until August 1939, and then I became a soldier.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Before you joined Schirach's staff, had you not served in the Armed Forces?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Up to joining Schirach in 1939 I had done 8 weeks' obligatory training in the Air Force.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Apart from that, you had no training?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Apart from that I had no military training.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Were you an officer?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I had not been an officer up to that time.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: So far as his other collaborators were concerned, did Schirach attach importance to their being officers or trained soldiers?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: So far as I know, Von Schirach did not care whether his collaborators were soldiers or officers, on the contrary, it was his view, as he told me repeatedly, that soldiers and officers, as far as he could see, were less suitable as youth leaders.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I do not want to go into the general question of the training of the Hitler Youth, but I wish to ask you one single question on this point, especially because you are a sports instructor by profession. It is a question about the training of the Hitler Youth in shooting. Were they trained with military weapons, or how were they trained in firing?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: The Hitler Youth were trained in shooting with air guns or small arms. They did not shoot with military weapons.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: In that case I will not put any further questions to you on the subject of uniform as these questions have already been clarified. But there is one other thing in which I am interested and that is the relationship to the Church: Do you know, Witness, whether the Defendant Von Schirach in 1937, that is in the issue of the Berlin paper, the Berliner Tageblatt R 14 January 1937, published an article written by his press adviser Gunther Kaufmann, headed "Can the Gap be Bridged"? That article, a copy of which I have before me, deals with a problem in which I am interested, and that is why I want to ask you: Do you know what Schirach made his press adviser write in that article on the question of whether the Hitler Youth leaders should consider the young people's need for church services or not?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I know the article.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You know it?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I also know the order issued by the Reich Youth Leader of that time stating that on Sundays there should be no Hitler Youth duty for all those boys and girls who wanted to attend church. Every boy and girl in the Hitler Youth at that time was supposed to be able to attend religious services of his or her own free will; and it was made part of the duty of the Hitler Youth leaders at the time to refrain from entering into any arguments or controversies about the Hitler Youth and the Church. He prohibited that.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, that is the main point of that article of 14 January 1937. But you know that the Defendant Schirach had certain difficulties with Hitler because of this article. Will you tell us briefly what you know about it?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: As soon as the agreement between the Church and the Hitler Youth was made, the article mentioned appeared in the Berliner Tageblatt . On the day that article appeared, Schirach was at a meeting in Rosenberg's office. Hitler called Schirach to the telephone at that time. Hitler took Schirach sternly to task, firstly, for making an agreement between the Church and the Hitler Youth and, secondly, for publishing this article. His intention was to cancel the agreement and to ban any further issue of the newspapers. Neither of these things happened.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did Schirach refuse to withdraw the article?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: So far as I know he did.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: In 1940 you went to Vienna with Schirach?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I did not.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: When did you go?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I went to Vienna for the first time in September 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Where had you been in the meantime?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I have already told you that I joined the Luftwaffe in August 1939 and served during that time as a service flying instructor in a Luftwaffe training school.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And you did not rejoin Schirach until 1941, and then in Henna?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes; I joined Schirach in Vienna in September 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: The highest dignitary of the Catholic Church in Vienna is Cardinal Innitzer, right?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know what Von Schirach's attitude to Cardinal Innitzer was? I will tell you at once why I am asking you this question; I want to know if it is true that Schirach objected to Cardinal Innitzer's being molested by the Hitler Youth, and what steps he took, et cetera.

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Schirach told me repeatedly that he would like to have a talk with Cardinal Innitzer, but that he was not allowed to do so, firstly, because of a decree issued by the former head of the Party Chancellery, Martin Bormann, prohibiting the Gauleiter from contacting Church dignitaries and, secondly, because Schirach knew that he himself was under surveillance.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Who, Schirach?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: That Schirach was under surveillance and thought that if he forced such a discussion, Bormann would be certain to know of it on the next day, which would have had most unpleasant consequences both for Schirach and Cardinal Innitzer. On the other hand, it was Schirach's view that Cardinal Innitzer also would certainly have liked to have a talk with Schirach and Schirach thought that certainly would not have been the case if Cardinal Innitzer had not known of his tolerant attitude toward the Church and the Christian religion. It is furthermore known to me-and I think this happened in the winter of 1944 to 1945-that Cardinal Innitzer was molested by youthful civilians while returning from mass. Cardinal Innitzer had the police find out the names of these youngsters, and they turned out to be Hitler Youth leaders. Schirach ordered the competent district leader of the Hitler Youth to him the same day, took him severely to task, and demanded that the youth leaders in question be relieved of their duties at once. As far as I know, this was actually done. I believe I also remember that Schirach had a letter of apology sent to Cardinal Innitzer, either personally or through one of his officials.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think we had better break off now. [The Tribunal adjourned until 28 May 1946 at 1000 hours.]

preamble: [The Defendant Von Schirach resumed the stand.]
